mappings.rationaleAI: Byzantium covers the empire from Constantinople's founding through its fall, including Justinian's reconquests, iconoclasm, and the Great Schism — directly relevant to the early Byzantine Empire (C) and the zenith/decline of Byzantium (F).

mappings.episodeTitle: The Roman Empire's Collapse in the 5th century
mappings.rationaleAI: The collapse of the Western Roman Empire directly addresses the end of the Western Empire and Germanic Volkerwanderung (B.1).
Constantine's reforms and founding of Constantinople address the origins of Byzantium in the late Roman Empire (C.1).
The Carolingian Renaissance under Charlemagne directly addresses Charlemagne's patronage of arts and learning (D.1.a) and intellectual life in the early Middle Ages (B.3.b).
Alcuin of York as Charlemagne's intellectual advisor directly addresses Carolingian education reform (D.1.a) and the cultural functions of monasticism (B.3.a).
The Arab conquests address the effects of the rise of Islam on western Europe (B.2.c) and Heraclius's losses to the Arabs (C.3.a).
Cnut as ruler of England, Denmark, and Norway directly addresses the Danish invasions and Anglo-Danish state (D.2.a).
The Danelaw directly addresses the Viking settlements in England, Alfred's resistance, and the reconquest of the Danelaw (D.2.a).

mappings.episodeTitle: Alfred and the Battle of Edington
mappings.rationaleAI: King Alfred and the Battle of Edington directly addresses Alfred's legal, administrative, and ecclesiastical policies (D.2.a).
Athelstan as the first king to unify England directly addresses Anglo-Saxon political unification in the 10th century (D.2.a).
The Volga Vikings cover Norse traders in Russia, the foundation of Kievan Rus, and trade with the Islamic world — directly relevant to the Viking Age (D.2.c) and the rise of the Rus (E.2.c).
The Picts and their merger with Scots under Kenneth MacAlpin directly addresses the development of the Kingdom of Scotland (D.2.b.i).
The Battle of Tours with Charles Martel defeating the Muslim invasion directly addresses the rise of the Carolingians under Charles Martel (B.2.b.iii) and effects of Islam on western Europe (B.2.c).
The Sassanid Empire's wars with Byzantium and its fall to the Arab conquest are relevant to Heraclius's wars with Persia (C.3.a).
The Plague of Justinian's devastating impact on the Byzantine Empire during Justinian's reign directly addresses events under Justinian and his successors (C.2.d).
The Great Schism of 1054 directly addresses the schism with Rome in the context of Byzantine decline (F.3).
The fall of Constantinople in 1453 addresses the ultimate end of Byzantine power, relevant to the broader arc of decline begun in section F.
David I of Scotland's introduction of feudalism and reformed monasticism directly addresses the development of the Kingdom of Scotland (D.2.b.i).
The Orkneyinga Saga chronicling Norse earls of Orkney directly addresses the Viking Age in Scandinavia and Norse settlement patterns (D.2.c).
The 12th-century Renaissance covering universities, scholastic philosophy, and Gothic architecture is relevant to the sociopolitical and economic structure of medieval Europe (D.6).
The Almoravid Empire's involvement in Iberia and the Reconquista is relevant to the growth of Christian states in northern Spain (D.5).
The Hanseatic League as a medieval trading confederation addresses localized commerce and economic development in medieval Europe (D.6).
Bohemia's Premyslid rulers and early Czech history directly address developments in Moravia and Bohemia (E.3.a).
The Venetian Empire's acquisition of territories after the Fourth Crusade addresses Western influences on Byzantium and the growth of Venice (F.3).
Julian the Apostate's attempt to restore paganism within the late Roman Empire addresses the origins of Byzantium in the reforms of the late empire (C.1).
Rome's enduring influence on European civilization including Christianity's adoption and translatio imperii is relevant to the amalgamation of Classical and Germanic cultures (B.3.a).
The Black Death's impact on medieval European society, economy, and demography is relevant to the sociopolitical and economic structure of medieval Europe (D.6).
The Battle of Crecy as an engagement of the Hundred Years' War addresses the Kingdom of France and Anglo-French relations under the Capetians and their successors (D.4).
